title: How the Hootin Heck do the Sim Bodies work
display_title: How the Hootin Heck do the Sim Bodies work
wikitext:
[]: How the Hootin' Heck do the Sim Bodies work?             Quote Post by Squigsquasher » Wed Feb 26, 2014 8:10 am OK, so here's the deal. It is a known fact that the pilots synchronize with the souls inside the Evangelions in order to pilot them. It is the melding of minds between the pilot and the soul within the Eva that makes the damn things work after all. Rei and Kaworu (neither of whom are human) are the only ones shown to be capable of piloting multiple Evangelions, so I assume that the pilots can only synchronize with 1 soul alone (their mother). Now, the Simulation Bodies are effectively Evangelions with most of their limbs amputated and strapped to life support machinery, to replicate an Evangelion as best as possible without actually using a potentially dangerous 80M tall furious maternal cyborg. But, the question is, how do they simulate the presence of the soul within (which is the whole point of synch tests after all)? Now, we know that it is impossible to create an artificial soul; it's explicitlly stated in fact. So, here's the possibilites I can see; 1: The Sim Bodies act as routers that allow for remote synchronization with the Pilot's actual Eva without getting into said Eva. So, they'll still be synchronizing with their mothers (or in the case of Rei, possibly with a fragment of herself, but let's not restart that debate) but without actually activating the fully functional Evas and thus avoiding the risk of RAWRRAWRRAWR. This seems like the most like solution. 2: The Sim Bodies run a similar thing to the Dummy Plugs; a replication of the actual souls in the form of an algorithm- not a full artificial soul, but close enough that it allows for accurate synchronization. The main problem I can see with this is that the Dummy Plug is only completed midway through the series, whereas the Sim Bodies are seen very early on. Unless of course the Sim Bodies use a more primitive version of the technology that can create a synchronizable image but not one that can pilot an Evangelion autonomously. 3: The Sim bodies actually contain souls of their own. Probably the most horrific possibility (can you imagine being trapped in a headless, limbless hunk of flesh suspended in freezing liquid for all eternity?) this is also the least likely- given that as previously mentioned, the only pilots shown to be able to pilot multiple Evas are Rei and Kaworu (who are incarnations of creator gods after all), I doubt that some random donor could synch with the pilots. Also, the Sim Bodies are shown not to have proper cores- rather they have an arrangement of 5 mini-cores (or Nucleoae as I believe Reichu called them). So, can anybody offer an explanation?

[]: Re: How the Hootin' Heck do the Sim Bodies work?             Quote Post by SoryuUberAlles » Wed Feb 26, 2014 8:32 am Squigsquasher wrote: 3: The Sim bodies actually contain souls of their own. Probably the most horrific possibility (can you imagine being trapped in a headless, limbless hunk of flesh suspended in freezing liquid for all eternity?) this is also the least likely- given that as previously mentioned, the only pilots shown to be able to pilot multiple Evas are Rei and Kaworu (who are incarnations of creator gods after all), I doubt that some random donor could synch with the pilots. Also, the Sim Bodies are shown not to have proper cores- rather they have an arrangement of 5 mini-cores (or Nucleoae as I believe Reichu called them).

[]:             Quote Post by Nuclear Lunchbox » Wed Feb 26, 2014 1:12 pm We knew that the cores of the Evangelions can be swapped out since episode 1. I think that they just take the cores out of the Evangelions, put them in the simulation bodies, and put them right back when they're done. It could explain why they're so paranoid about anything getting into the Pribnow box.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Wed Feb 26, 2014 1:57 pm I like the primitive dummy plug solution (or rather, this experiment, much like the pilot swap in 14, is a step towards developing the final dummy plug), and assume the simulation bodies are just failed evas that were salvaged for additional testing rather than sent immediately to the boneyard.

[]:             Quote Post by Kendrix » Wed Feb 26, 2014 3:13 pm NemZ wrote: I like the primitive dummy plug solution (or rather, this experiment, much like the pilot swap in 14, is a step towards developing the final dummy plug), and assume the simulation bodies are just failed evas that were salvaged for additional testing rather than sent immediately to the boneyard.

[]:             Quote Post by SoryuUberAlles » Wed Feb 26, 2014 6:17 pm Now that I think about it… I came into this assuming that the Simulation Bodies would not be, mechanically speaking, anything like the Dummy System. The Dummy System seems to be related to Rei/Kaworu clones that generate something that goes inside the the artificial Dummy Plugs, that link to EVAs. Simulation Bodies are things like EVAS that 'real' Entry Plugs for people with 'real' souls are linked with. NemZ wrote: I like the primitive dummy plug solution (or rather, this experiment, much like the pilot swap in 14, is a step towards developing the final dummy plug), and assume the simulation bodies are just failed evas that were salvaged for additional testing rather than sent immediately to the boneyard.

[]:             Quote Post by Stratomeister » Wed Feb 26, 2014 7:25 pm Nuclear Lunchbox wrote: We knew that the cores of the Evangelions can be swapped out since episode 1. I think that they just take the cores out of the Evangelions, put them in the simulation bodies, and put them right back when they're done. It could explain why they're so paranoid about anything getting into the Pribnow box.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Wed Feb 26, 2014 8:04 pm What I think I'm getting at is that simulation bodies are probably real (broken) evas with dummy cores installed. This makes sense as the core souls have been studied even more extensively than the pilots and so replicating them (especially since they don't need to do anything but exist) is a sensible first step towards using the same technology from the other end to create the dummy plugs.

[]:             Quote Post by Nuclear Lunchbox » Wed Feb 26, 2014 8:11 pm Stratomeister wrote: Was it actually stated somewhere that the cores were physically removed from the Evangelions? I always just assumed that some kind of program was rewritten or edited whenever the cores were reconfigured.

[]:             Quote Post by K40s » Wed Feb 26, 2014 8:57 pm After seeing NTE I'm imagining that the cores are removable and they simply put the core with the mother's soul in the dummy body for a safer test.

[]:             Quote Post by Stratomeister » Wed Feb 26, 2014 9:18 pm Nuclear Lunchbox wrote: In NME, the cores are absolutely physical. Remember 2.22? However, in NGE, the source material for the question, I don't know. However, I don't think that the physicality of the core would affect its transfer.

[]:             Quote Post by Nuclear Lunchbox » Wed Feb 26, 2014 9:24 pm That would indeed be a possible solution that I am proposing, yes.
